questions.question: Explain Gaussian noise and its mathematical representation in image processing.
questions.hint: Remember the probability density function of Gaussian distribution

questions.question: What is salt-and-pepper noise? Explain its characteristics and how it affects image pixels.
questions.hint: This noise has only two possible intensity values
keyPoints: Appears as white and black dots randomly
Pixels take only maximum (255) or minimum (0) values
Caused by faulty memory locations or analog-to-digital converter errors
Also called shot noise or spike noise

questions.question: Describe the arithmetic mean filter for noise removal. Mention its advantages and limitations.
questions.hint: This is a spatial domain linear filter
keyPoints: Arithmetic mean filter computes average of neighborhood pixels
Formula: R = (1/mn) Σ f(x,y) for all pixels in neighborhood

questions.question: Explain the working of a median filter for image restoration. Why is it effective for salt-and-pepper noise?
keyPoints: Median filter sorts neighborhood pixel values and selects middle value
Does not create new pixel values

questions.question: Compare Gaussian noise and salt-and-pepper noise with respect to their characteristics and suitable restoration methods.
questions.hint: Focus on differences in nature and restoration approaches
keyPoints: Gaussian: random intensity variations following normal distribution
Salt-and-pepper: discrete random white and black pixels
Gaussian noise: arithmetic mean or Wiener filter works well
Gaussian affects all pixel values continuously
Salt-and-pepper affects pixels with extreme values only

questions.question: Define periodic noise in images. How does it differ from random noise?
questions.hint: Periodic noise can be filtered using notch filters
keyPoints: Periodic noise has regular, repeating patterns
Caused by electrical interference or mechanical issues
Can be identified in frequency domain
Appears as discrete impulses in frequency domain
Periodic noise is deterministic while random is stochastic

questions.question: Derive the mathematical formulation of the Wiener filter for image restoration in the presence of Gaussian noise.
questions.hint: Wiener filter is optimal linear filter for MSE minimization
keyPoints: Wiener filter minimizes mean square error
Combines degradation function and noise characteristics
S_f is power spectrum of original image
S_η is power spectrum of noise
Assumes noise is uncorrelated with image
Reduces to inverse filtering when no noise

questions.question: Discuss the various spatial domain filters used for restoration in the presence of noise. Explain their working principles and compare their performance.
questions.hint: Include both linear and non-linear filters
keyPoints: Arithmetic mean: smooths but blurs edges
Harmonic mean: good for salt noise
Contraharmonic mean: preserves edges, removes specific noise
Median filter: best for impulse noise

questions.question: Explain the model of image degradation and restoration process. How does restoration differ from enhancement?
keyPoints: Restoration aims to recover original image
Restoration is objective process based on degradation model
Enhancement uses techniques like contrast stretching

questions.question: Describe how periodic noise is reduced using frequency domain filtering. Explain the use of bandreject and notch filters.
questions.hint: Periodic noise has localized frequency components
keyPoints: Periodic noise appears as discrete spikes in frequency domain
Bandreject filter removes range of frequencies
Notch filter removes specific frequency components
Ideal bandreject: passes all except blocked band
Notch filters target specific interference patterns
Requires identification of noise peaks in spectrum

questions.question: Explain the geometric mean filter. Derive its formula and compare it with arithmetic mean filter.
keyPoints: Better than arithmetic mean for edge preservation
Formula involves product instead of sum
Reduces to arithmetic mean in log domain
More sensitive to zero pixel values
Better for images with low noise

questions.question: Consider a 3×3 image patch with pixel values: [12, 14, 16; 18, 100, 22; 24, 26, 28]. Apply a 3×3 median filter to this patch and determine the output central pixel value.
questions.hint: Sort all neighborhood pixels and find median

questions.question: Given a 3×3 neighborhood with pixel values: [10, 20, 30; 40, 50, 60; 70, 80, 90], calculate the output using arithmetic mean filter.
questions.hint: Apply the arithmetic mean formula to all pixels in neighborhood
keyPoints: Arithmetic mean formula: (1/9) × Σ pixel values
No change since values were uniformly increasing

questions.question: A 3×3 image patch corrupted by salt-and-pepper noise has values: [0, 255, 30; 45, 0, 70; 90, 110, 255]. After applying a 3×3 median filter, determine: (a) The output central pixel value (b) How many salt and pepper pixels are removed.
questions.hint: Sort values and find middle element
keyPoints: All 3 noise pixels are removed in output
Median filter effective for impulse noise
